claim: YOLO provider dispatch and evidence dispatch are implemented in a dirty working tree with comprehensive provider-specific tool configuration and boundary enforcement, but lack real-world stress testing evidence.

evidence.summary: buildProviderInvocation implements dispatch for Claude, Codex, and custom providers with provider-specific tool configuration
evidence.why: This is the core provider dispatch implementation that handles tool capability per provider.

evidence.summary: Claude provider supports --tools, --allowedTools, --disallowedTools, --permission-mode, --settings, --max-budget-usd, and session persistence flags
evidence.why: Shows full tool capability surface for Claude provider that must be preserved under harness boundary.

evidence.summary: Codex provider uses exec mode with configurable sandbox (workspace-write default), approval policy (never default), model selection, and output-last-message file capture
evidence.why: Codex provider has different tool surface that must not drift from Claude assumptions.

evidence.summary: Custom provider executes arbitrary shell commands with external sandbox mode and stdout capture
evidence.why: Custom provider is the most flexible and highest-risk dispatch path.

evidence.summary: buildDemandEvidenceDispatchPlan creates agent tasks (explorer, cross-checker, verifier) with boundary-controlled execution policy and artifact root
evidence.why: Evidence dispatch is the core protocol for gathering project facts before PRD.

evidence.summary: executionConfig normalizes agent_tool_profile to boundary/research/full and sets claude_tools=default, claude_allowed_tools=empty, claude_disallowed_tools=empty, claude_permission_mode=bypassPermissions
evidence.why: Shows how evidence mode controls tool capability per provider while preserving boundary.

evidence.summary: buildBoundarySnapshot and diffBoundarySnapshots detect project file mutations by comparing git ls-files and directory walks before/after agent execution
evidence.why: Boundary enforcement is the primary guard against evidence agents modifying target project files.

evidence.summary: normalizeAgentProvider maps provider aliases (anthropic→claude, openai→codex, shell/local→custom) and handles auto/null defaults
evidence.why: Provider normalization prevents cross-provider model leakage at the contract level.

evidence.summary: buildAgentAdapterCapabilities defines per-provider capability contracts including sandbox, approval_policy, file_write, shell_exec, and budget_limit
evidence.why: Capability contract is the formal boundary between provider assumptions and harness expectations.

evidence.summary: Comprehensive tests cover provider-specific dispatch (Claude, Codex, custom), tool profiles (boundary, research, full), boundary enforcement (git and non-git), external research requirements, and evidence scope validation
evidence.why: Test coverage shows the implementation is well-specified but all tests use fake agent mocks.

evidence.summary: Test verifies codex dispatch does not inherit claude default model or force read-only sandbox, confirming provider isolation
evidence.why: Evidence that provider-specific assumptions are separated in tests.

evidence.summary: Test verifies claude dispatch keeps default tools available and relies on boundary enforcement, confirming tool preservation
evidence.why: Evidence that tool capability preservation is tested.

evidence.summary: inspectDemandTriage triggers careful route with cross_check evidence policy for brownfield existing projects with technical or data risk
evidence.why: Demand router is the policy layer that decides when evidence mode is required.

evidence.summary: runDemandEvidenceDispatchRuntime spawns provider agents, captures JSON output, normalizes results, checks boundary violations, and updates PRD readiness
evidence.why: Runtime execution is the integration point between provider dispatch and evidence gathering.

evidence.summary: Dirty working tree with 50+ modified files and untracked evidence dispatch implementation (src/demand/evidence-dispatch.ts, src/demand/router.ts, __tests__/demand-evidence-dispatch.test.ts)
evidence.why: Confirms the claim that evidence dispatch exists in an uncommitted working tree.

evidence.summary: Recent commits show demand PRD workflow development but no evidence of real-world stress testing with Claude -p agents
evidence.why: Missing evidence of production stress testing supports the assumption claim.

assumptions: Provider configuration preserves full tool capability under harness boundary - this is asserted in code and tests but not verified with real Claude -p agent runs.
Cross-provider model leakage is prevented by normalizeAgentProvider aliases - provider isolation is tested only with mocks, not real agent execution.
Boundary enforcement (file snapshot diff) is sufficient to detect mutations - no evidence of bypass testing with privileged tools.
Evidence dispatch works in production demand workflows - implementation exists but no real usage logs or artifacts.
risks: A provider-specific tool assumption (like Codex sandbox modes or Claude permission modes) could silently weaken dispatch or break real agent calls if the mock tests don't cover actual provider behavior.
Cross-provider model leakage could occur if normalizeAgentProvider misses an alias or if config.ai.provider and config.ai.executor diverge in real usage.
Boundary enforcement might fail to detect mutations if git state is inconsistent or if agents have write access to allowed_write_roots that overlap project files.
Evidence dispatch runtime could fail to parse agent JSON or miss scope violations if real providers return unexpected output formats.
missing: Real-world Claude -p agent execution logs or artifacts showing evidence dispatch works outside mock tests.
Stress test results showing boundary enforcement detects mutations with full/research tool profiles.
Cross-provider isolation evidence showing Codex and custom providers don't inherit Claude tool configuration.
Production demand session artifacts (.yolo/demand/*/session.json) showing evidence dispatch completed successfully.
Provider adapter contract validation results from inspectAgentAdapterContract showing no blockers in real environment.

result.notes: Code and test evidence confirms provider dispatch and evidence dispatch are implemented with comprehensive provider-specific tool configuration and boundary enforcement. However, all tests use fake agent mocks, and there is no evidence of real-world stress testing or production usage. Cross-check verification is recommended to validate assumptions about tool capability preservation and cross-provider isolation with actual provider execution.
